level1.title: Place Value to 10,000
level1.goal: Understand thousands/hundreds/tens/ones, represent numbers in multiple ways, and round sensibly.

g3-l1-big-idea.title: Big idea
g3-l1-big-idea.content: Place value is a *map*: each step left is 10x bigger (ones -> tens -> hundreds -> thousands). When kids can say a number in parts (6 thousands, 3 hundreds...), comparing and rounding become calm and logical.

g3-l1-notice.title: What to notice
g3-l1-notice.content:
* Compares thousands first, then hundreds, then tens.
* Reads 4-digit numbers smoothly (no "sixty-three hundred" unless intended).
* Can explain rounding as "which benchmark is it closer to?"
* Understands digit value (the 5 in 7,532 is 500, not 5).

g3-l1-home.title: Try at home
g3-l1-home.content:
* **Game A (core idea): "Bundle 10, Bundle 10"**
Make 100 with 10 bundles of 10 (straws, sticks, coins). Then trade 10 bundles for 1 "hundred" card.
* **Game B (second representation): "Number Builder Cards"**
Write 0-9 on small cards. Draw 4 cards to make a number. Ask: "What's the biggest you can make? The smallest? What changes if you swap two cards?"

g3-l1-videos.title: Optional videos
g3-l1-videos.content: Search for place value to 10,000 for kids on YouTube. Search for rounding to nearest 10/100/1000 number line on YouTube. Practice with Khan Academy: place value & rounding.

level2.title: Add/Subtract Within 1,000
level2.goal: Add and subtract within 1,000 using place-value reasoning, regrouping, and estimation.

g3-l2-big-idea.title: Big idea
g3-l2-big-idea.content: Adding and subtracting within 1,000 is about *trading* (10 ones <-> 1 ten, 10 tens <-> 1 hundred). Estimation helps kids feel: "Is my answer sensible?"

g3-l2-notice.title: What to notice
g3-l2-notice.content:
* Uses place value language: "300 + 40 + 7".
* Trades calmly when needed (not random borrowing).
* Checks answers with estimation ("about 600").
* Can tell whether a story needs +, -, or both.

g3-l2-home.title: Try at home
g3-l2-home.content:
* **"Trade Bank"**: Use coins/buttons. 10 ones trade for 1 ten card; 10 tens trade for 1 hundred card. Make numbers, then trade to subtract.
* **"Estimate Jar"**: Guess about how many in a small jar. Then count by tens. Compare estimate vs actual.

g3-l2-videos.title: Optional videos
g3-l2-videos.content: Search for addition and subtraction within 1000 base ten blocks. Search for regrouping with zeros subtraction for kids. Practice with Khan Academy: add & subtract within 1,000.

level3.title: Multiply & Divide Within 100
level3.goal: Understand multiplication and division as equal groups and arrays, and build fluent strategies.

g3-l3-big-idea.title: Big idea
g3-l3-big-idea.content: Multiplication is "equal groups," and division is "equal sharing" or "how many groups." Arrays help kids *see* the structure, not just memorize facts.

g3-l3-notice.title: What to notice
g3-l3-notice.content:
* Counts by groups instead of counting one-by-one.
* Uses known facts to get new ones (double/halve, 5s and 10s).
* Reads 6x4 as "6 groups of 4" and "4 groups of 6."
* For division, can explain what the answer *means* (per group vs number of groups).

g3-l3-home.title: Try at home
g3-l3-home.content:
* **"Array Snacks"**: Put crackers in rows (e.g., 4 rows of 6). Ask: "How many? What's the equation?"
* **"Share & Check"**: Share 18 grapes among 3 bowls. Count each bowl. Then ask: "What if we had 6 bowls?"

g3-l3-videos.title: Optional videos
g3-l3-videos.content: Search for arrays and equal groups for kids multiplication. Search for division as sharing for kids. Practice with Khan Academy: multiplication & division.

level4.title: Fractions as Numbers
level4.goal: Understand fractions as equal parts of a whole and as numbers on the number line, including equivalence and comparisons.

g3-l4-big-idea.title: Big idea
g3-l4-big-idea.content: A fraction is a number made from *equal parts*. "Three fourths" means 3 pieces where each piece is 1/4. Number lines help fractions feel real and ordered.

g3-l4-notice.title: What to notice
g3-l4-notice.content:
* Checks parts are equal before naming the fraction.
* Can explain numerator vs denominator in simple language ("how many parts" / "what size parts").
* Places fractions on a number line using the partitions.
* Compares fractions by reasoning (same denominator -> compare numerators).

g3-l4-home.title: Try at home
g3-l4-home.content:
* **"Fold & Label"**: Fold paper into 2, 4, 8 equal parts. Shade some parts and label (3/8).
* **"Snack Fractions"**: Cut fruit into equal slices. Ask: "If we eat 2 out of 6 slices, what fraction did we eat?"

g3-l4-videos.title: Optional videos
g3-l4-videos.content: Search for fractions on a number line grade 3. Search for equivalent fractions visual models for kids. Practice with Khan Academy: fractions.

level5.title: Measurement & Geometry
level5.goal: Measure and reason about area and perimeter, read time problems, interpret scaled graphs, and classify quadrilaterals.

g3-l5-big-idea.title: Big idea
g3-l5-big-idea.content: Perimeter is the *distance around*, area is the *space covered*. Counting squares builds meaning, and multiplication makes it efficient. Graphs and shape sorting are about noticing structure.

g3-l5-notice.title: What to notice
g3-l5-notice.content:
* Counts only the *outside* for perimeter (not interior lines).
* For area, counts square units without gaps or overlaps.
* Uses rows x columns for rectangles.
* Reads the scale on a bar graph before answering.
* Understands "square is a rectangle" as a category idea.

g3-l5-home.title: Try at home
g3-l5-home.content:
* **"Tape Perimeter"**: Use string/tape to outline a book. Measure the string with a ruler (or count hand spans).
* **"Area Tiles"**: Cover a small rectangle with coins or tiles. Count rows and columns, then multiply.

g3-l5-videos.title: Optional videos
g3-l5-videos.content: Search for area and perimeter for kids grade 3. Search for elapsed time number line for kids. Search for scaled bar graphs for kids. Practice with Khan Academy: measurement & data.

balloons.template: {name} has {a} balloons. {pronoun} buys {b} more, then gives {c} away.
balloons.question: How many balloons does {name} have now?

library.template: A library has {a} books. They buy {b} new books, then donate {c} old books.
library.question: How many books does the library have now?

farmerApples.template: A farmer picks {a} apples in the morning and {b} apples in the afternoon. {pronoun2} sells {c} apples at the market.
farmerApples.question: How many apples does the farmer have left?

stickers.template: {name} collects {a} stickers. {pronoun} trades away {b} stickers and then gets {c} new ones.
stickers.question: How many stickers does {name} have now?

busStops.template: A bus has {a} passengers. At the first stop, {b} people get off. At the second stop, {c} people get on.
busStops.question: How many passengers are on the bus now?

savings.template: {name} saves ${a}. {pronoun} spends ${b} on a toy and then earns ${c} doing chores.
savings.question: How much money does {name} have now?

birds.template: There are {a} birds in a tree. {b} birds fly away, then {c} more birds join them.
birds.question: How many birds are in the tree now?

candies.template: {name} has {a} candies. {pronoun} gets {b} more from a friend, then eats {c} candies.
candies.question: How many candies does {name} have now?
